coredata.dc:title: Insight on the corrosion inhibition performance of psidium guajava linn leaves extract
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yundong
Guo, Lei
She, Yuanbin
coredata.dc:description: This work multi-angle explored the anti-corrosion nature and mechanism of psidium guajava linn leaves extract for copper in H2SO4 environment.In this study, we used a simple and eco-friendly extraction way to acquire psidium guajava linn leaves extract (PGLLE). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was executed to determine the active functional groups in PGLLE. Quantum chemical calculations effectively forecast the anti-corrosion characteristic of the four active substances in PGLLE. The results of molecular dynamics simulation predicted the stabilized adsorption configurations of the four substances in PGLLE on the Cu (111). Surface topography analysis can intuitively demonstrate that PGLLE effectually inhibits Cu corrosion. The data of electrochemical experiments show that the anti-corrosion capacity of PGLLE increases with the increase of the concentration in a certain interval. As the temperature hoists at definite temperature range, PGLLE can still be efficaciously adsorbed at the Cu surface, showing high-quality corrosion inhibition capabilities. Psidium guajava linn leaves can exist widely in nature, so it has great economic value as the inhibitor. Besides, PGLLE basically does not pollute the ecosystem when used as inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper
Psidium guajava linn leaves extract
Corrosion inhibitor
Sulfuric acid
Langmuir model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Psidium guajava linn leaves extraction and FTIR

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Education of Guizhou province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21706195, 22062022), and the Foundation of the Department of Education of Guizhou province (QJHKY2020-067, KY[2018]030).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FTIR of psidium guajava linn leaves extract.
The molecular structures of the four main ingredient of psidium guajava linn leaves extract.
The molecular structures and ESP diagram of the four primary components of psidium guajava linn leaves extract.
The electron cloud distribution map of the front molecular orbitals of the four main components of psidium guajava linn leaves extract.
(a) The μ value and (b) energy gap value of the four main components of PGLLE.
The top and side views of the four primary components of psidium guajava linn leaves extract on the Cu(111) surface.

(a) Surface topography of copper samples after grinding on sandpaper, (b) The morphology of Cu steep in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 containing 600 mg/L psidium guajava linn leaves extract for 24 h at 298 K, (c) Surface morphology of Cu steep in 0.5 M H2SO4 without psidium guajava linn leaves extract for 24 h.

(a) The morphology of Cu steep in 0.5 M H2SO4 containing 600 mg/L psidium guajava linn leaves extract for 24 h. (b) The morphology of Cu steep in 0.5 M H2SO4 environment for 24 h without psidium guajava linn leaves extract.
Average roughness value and 2D contour map of copper surface.
Cu electrode immersed in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 containing diverse concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract: (a) polarization curve diagram and (b) open circuit potential diagram.
Copper electrodes steep at 0.5 M H2SO4 with unlike concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract: (a) Nyquist plot and (b) Bode plot.
Equivalent circuit plots to fit EIS: (a) with Warburg impedance, (b) without Warburg impedance.
Cu electrode immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4: (a) Polarization curve graph and (b) OCP graph.
Cu electrodes immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4: (a) Nyquist diagram and (b) Bode diagram.
Cu electrode immersed in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 with diverse concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract (a) Polarization curve graph and (b) OCP graph.
Copper electrodes are steep in 0.5 M H2SO4 at various temperatures with diverse concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract: (a) Nyquist plots and (b) Bode diagram.
Langmuir adsorption isotherm model.
Four different isothermal models: (a) Temkin, (b) Frumkin, (c) Freundlich, and (d) El-Awady.
Polarization curves parameters of Cu electrodes immersed in various concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract.
EIS parameters of Cu electrodes steep in 0.5 M H2SO4 with various concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract.
Tafel parameters of Cu with unlike concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract at diverse temperatures.
EIS parameters of Cu with diverse concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract at diverse temperatures.

head.ce:title: Insight on the corrosion inhibition performance of psidium guajava linn leaves extract

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310014, Zhejiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry Synthesis Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310014, Zhejiang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work multi-angle explored the anti-corrosion nature and mechanism of psidium guajava linn leaves extract for copper in H2SO4 environment.In this study, we used a simple and eco-friendly extraction way to acquire psidium guajava linn leaves extract (PGLLE). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was executed to determine the active functional groups in PGLLE. Quantum chemical calculations effectively forecast the anti-corrosion characteristic of the four active substances in PGLLE. The results of molecular dynamics simulation predicted the stabilized adsorption configurations of the four substances in PGLLE on the Cu (111). Surface topography analysis can intuitively demonstrate that PGLLE effectually inhibits Cu corrosion. The data of electrochemical experiments show that the anti-corrosion capacity of PGLLE increases with the increase of the concentration in a certain interval. As the temperature hoists at definite temperature range, PGLLE can still be efficaciously adsorbed at the Cu surface, showing high-quality corrosion inhibition capabilities. Psidium guajava linn leaves can exist widely in nature, so it has great economic value as the inhibitor. Besides, PGLLE basically does not pollute the ecosystem when used as inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion issue of metal has caused great troubles to the progress of human culture from ancient times to the present [1–7]. How to effectively overcome or reduce the corrosion problem of metal materials becomes an urgent problem for corrosion scientists. The metals corrosion has brought a series of serious problems to the world, including environmental pollution, safety accidents, and maintenance costs of metal equipment, etc [8–19]. Therefore, corrosion researchers have developed a series of effective treatment methods for metal corrosion [20–24]. Among them, inhibitors have become one of the irreplaceable protection way of metal corrosion due to their advantages of simple operation, significant effects, and high economic value [25–28].

In this research work, we are studying metallic copper. As a valuable non-ferrous metal, Cu has acted a huge character in promoting the progress and development of human society. In ancient times when productivity was low, copper could be used to make weapons, coins, cooking utensils, musical instruments, and so on. In modern civilization where smelting technology is quite developed, the purity of copper can be greatly improved. It can be widely used in microelectronics, cables, heat exchangers and other fields. This is due to the relative corrosion resistance, good ductility, good thermal conductivity and good electrical conductivity of copper [29]. However, when exposed to severe service environment for a long time, the copper substrate will also be corroded by corrosive media. Therefore, when removing the corrosion substance at the Cu surface, it is often requisite to add a certain amount of inhibitors to the pickling medium. This allows the copper substrate to be effectively protected.

Organic inhibitor usually contain their own particular feature. For example, it contains unsaturated functional groups, and heteroatoms (nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur) [1,19,30–36]. Although organic inhibitor can efficaciously adsorb at the metal interface to control the corrosion of the corrosive medium to the metal substrate, it also has many disadvantages. For example, many corrosion inhibitors themselves are very toxic and can generate great detriment to the ecosystem. Therefore, the development of green inhibitors has become a way to conform to the trend of the environmental protection era [18]. In recent years, green corrosion inhibitors developed by corrosion workers include oral medicines, edible flavors, amino acids, and plant extracts [37]. Tan et al. [38] studied the anti-corrosion nature of the Montelukast Sodium (asthma drug) on Cu in H2SO4 medium. Electrochemical test data show that the Montelukast Sodium is 50 mg/L, its anti-corrosion ability can approach 99%. When the temperature of the test system rises to 313 K, the η of Montelukast Sodium can still reach about 91%. Zhou et al. [39] studied the anti-corrosion character of two oral drugs (Phenothiazine) on copper in H2SO4 medium. The CPE reaches 400 mg/L, its η can reach more than 97%. XPS test results show that the formation of S-Cu bonds and N-Cu bonds is detected at the copper surface. Wu et al. [40] researched the anti-corrosion character of orange peel extracts as a degradable inhibitor for magnesium alloy in a neutral environment. EIS test data show that Orange peel extracts is 0.03 g/L, its η can reach more than 85%. Sedik et al. [41] studied the anti-corrosion nature of Dardagan Fruit extract on steel in a HCl medium. The Dardagan Fruit extract is 3000 ppm, the η of Dardagan Fruit extract is about 92%. Zhang et al. [16] researched the anti-corrosion feature of Davidia involucrata leaf extract on Cu in the H2SO4. In a certain temperature range, Davidia involucrata leaf extract is 300 mg/L, its η can be maintained at about 90%.

In this work, we used the H2O extraction way to obtain Psidium guajava linn leaves extract. Psidium guajava linn exists widely in nature, so Psidium guajava linn leaves have high economic value as a corrosion inhibitor. In addition, the Psidium guajava linn leaves extract obtained by the water extraction method can be dissolved in a sulfuric acid corrosive environment, so that the Psidium guajava linn leaves extract can be uniformly adsorbed at the Cu surface. In addition, we also used theoretical calculation methods to deeply explore the quantum chemical parameters of PGLLE. In addition, we have proved the anti-corrosion character of Psidium guajava linn leaves extract at copper in sulfuric acid media from multiple angles through surface morphology analysis, electrochemical experiments and adsorption isotherm simulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Psidium guajava linn leaves extraction and FTIR
ce:section.ce:para: A sufficient amount of Psidium guajava linn leaves is collected and rinsed with ultrapure water 3 times until the leaves are clean. Dry the psidium guajava linn leaves naturally, and then put the psidium guajava linn leaves in the open air until the psidium guajava linn leaves are dry. The dried psidium guajava linn leaves are placed in a pulverizer and crushed into powder for later use. Weigh 300 g of psidium guajava linn leaves powder into a 1 L beaker, and then add 0.5 L of ultrapure H2O to the beaker. It is heated and concentrated until the water in the beaker is concentrated to about 100 mL, and then filtered with gauze. Put the filtered concentrate into a 100 mL small beaker, and when the temperature cools to room temperature, transfer the small beaker to the refrigerator. After 12 h, when the concentrate in the small beaker is completely solidified, immediately transfer the beaker to the freeze-drying oven. After freezing in the freeze-drying oven for 36 h, we can get the psidium guajava linn leaves extract. Take a small amount of PGLLE for infrared spectroscopy test. The remaining part is used for electrochemical experiment testing. In order to authenticate the ingredients in the psidium guajava linn leaves extract, we performed Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet iS50).

By referring to the references [42–45] of the ingredients in psidium guajava linn leaves and combining the infrared spectrum data in Fig. 1 . We can judge that psidium guajava linn leaves extract contains (S)-2-amino-5-guanidnopentanoic acid (AGPA), 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxy-4H-chromen-4-one (DDTC), (S)-2-amino-3-phenylpropanoic acid (APPA), and (2R,3S)-2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)chroman-3,5,7-trial (DPCT). Their chemical structures were listed in Fig. 2 .

Psidium guajava linn leaves extract is dissolved in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 and prepared into 50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, 200 mg/L, 400 mg/L, and 600 mg/L test solutions. The 0.5 M H2SO4 was used as the blank solution.

Quantum chemistry calculations and molecular dynamics simulations were used to calculate the four components AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT in Psidium guajava linn leaves extract. Materials Studio software was used to calculate the dipole moment, electrostatic potential diagram (ESP), and electron cloud distribution of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT. The Geometry Optimization as the calculation task, GGA and PBE selected as the Functional, the Basis set is DNP.

Molecular dynamics simulations were used to calculate the stabilized adsorption patterns of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT on the Cu(111) interface. The specific calculation parameters are as follows: the 20 Å vacuum layer is set to guarantee the adsorption of inhibitor molecules at the Cu(111) surface. Fill the 20 Å vacuum layer with an AGPA, DDTC, APPA, or DPCT and 400 water molecules. The calculate time was 800 ps. Force field is the COMPASS and time step is 1 fs.

The Cu sample was cut into 0.6 cm × 0.6 cm × 0.6 cm size for scanning electron microscopy (TESCAN MIRA 3 FE) and atomic force microscopy (MFP-3D-BIO). The Cu sample is meticulously burnished at 180 to 5000 # sandpaper. After polishing and smoothing, the Cu sample was diped in a 0.5 M H2SO4 with and lacking 600 mg/L Psidium guajava linn leaves extract for 24 h at 298 K. After the immersion, the surface of the Cu sample is cleaned and tested.

The electrochemical test of this work was tested using a traditional three-electrode cell. Copper was used as working electrode (WE), saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as reference electrode (RE), and platinum slice was used as counter electrode. Before the open circuit potential test, the Cu electrode is polished on 180 to 3000 mesh sandpaper, and the electrochemical experiment test is carried out after the polishing is flat. The test time of the open circuit potential is set to 1800 s to pledge that the interface of the copper electrode has reached a steady condition. The initial potential of the Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) test was a stable EOCP with a frequency ranging from 100,000 Hz to 0.01 Hz, and the excitation signal was an AC voltage value of 5 mV. The polarization test range of the polarization curve is EOCP ± 0.25 V with 0.5 mV/s scan rate.

Fig. 1 presents the FTIR of psidium guajava linn leaves extract. The peak at 3276 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H. The 2930 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the C-H bond. The peak at 1600 cm−1 is due to the C = C bond or C = N bond. The peaks at 1517 cm−1 and 1438 cm−1 are caused by the arrow bending vibrations of –CH2- and –CH3. The 1375 cm−1 is caused by the –CH3 plane rocking vibration. The peak at 1120 cm−1 is caused by the vibration of the C-O bond. The peak at 1035 cm−1 is caused by C-H vibration. Through infrared spectrum analysis, it can be found that psidium guajava linn leaves extract contains N and O.Therefore, it has potential as an inhibitor.

Fig. 3 shows the optimized structural formulas of AGPA, DDTC, APPA and DPCT and the corresponding electrostatic potential diagrams. In Fig. 3, the optimized structures of AGPA, DDTC, and APPA are almost on the same plane, which helps them form a more orderly isolation film on the Cu interface. Besides, the electrostatic potential diagrams of AGPA, DDTC, APPA and DPCT are composed of blue and red regions, respectively. The red and blue areas indicate nucleophilic and electrophilic nature, respectively [46]. The red district in AGPA are principally distributed on the amino and carboxyl groups. The red area in DDTC is on the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups. The red area in APPA is mainly distributed at the benzene ring and O atom. The red area in DPCT is distributed onto part of the hydroxyl groups. This indicates that these red areas are the main active adsorption sites when adsorbing at the Cu surface.

Fig. 4 shows the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT, respectively. HOMO orbital stands for the electron-donating capacity of molecule, and the LUMO orbital stands for the electron-acquiring capacity of a molecule [7]. In Fig. 4, it can be clearly found that the electron cloud of HOMO of APPA and DPCT is almost uniformly distributed at the whole molecule. This manifests that these active adsorption sites can provide electrons and metals to form chemical adsorption.

Fig. 5 (a) and (b) show the dipole and energy gap values of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT, respectively. The μ values of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT are 5.07 D, 1.12 D, 1.69 D and 1.42 D in Fig. 5 (a), respectively. Generally speaking, a large dipole moment value can often show higher corrosion inhibition performance [47]. Therefore, the anti-corrosion ability of AGPA is better than the other three molecules. The energy values of the HOMO orbitals of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT are − 5.98 eV, −5.91 eV, −6.26 eV, and − 5.68 eV in Fig. 5(b), respectively. The higher the energy value of the HOMO orbital, the stronger the electron donating capacity of the corrosion inhibitor, and the easier it is to exhibit high corrosion inhibition performance.

Fig. 6 shows the stable configuration of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT after adsorption at the Cu (111) interface. AGPA, DDTC, and APPA are adsorbed in parallel at the Cu (111) surface, while DPCT uses a parallel adsorption on one side and a vertical adsorption on the other side. The binding energy (Ebinding ) of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT after adsorption at Cu (111) interface are calculated by formulas (1) and (2) [48–50]:
(1)
E
int e r a c t
=
E
tot
-
(
E
subs
+
E
inh
)
(2)
E
binding
= -
E
int e r a c t

The Ebinding values of AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT are − 321.3 kJ/mol, −590.2 kJ/mol, −346.1 kJ/mol, and − 508.2 kJ/mol, respectively. Therefore, DPCT has the strongest adsorption capacity at the Cu(111) surface.

Fig. 7 (a), (b) and (c) respectively show the surface topography of copper samples under disparate test conditions. Fig. 7 (a) is the surface character of the copper sample after burnish with 5000 # sandpaper. There are obvious scratches on the total Cu surface after polishing. Fig. 7(c) shows the copper sample morphology after immersing it in 0.5 M H2SO4 at 298 K for 24 h. It can be clearly found that there are many pits and small holes at the entire copper base. Fig. 7(b) shows that the Cu sample was immersed in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 solution for 24 h and contained 600 mg/L psidium guajava linn leaves extract. Comparing Fig. 7(c), it can be clearly found that the interface of the Cu sample is still correspondingly flat, so it can be intuitively judged that psidium guajava linn leaves extract can control copper corrosion in H2SO4 medium.

Fig. 8 (a) and (b) respectively show the morphology of Cu samples immersed in 0.5 M sulfuric acid with and without 600 mg/L psidium guajava linn leaves extract. Fig. 9 presents the 2D contour maps of the Cu specimens at various experimental conditions and the corresponding roughness values (Ra). The copper sample was immersed in a 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid for 24 h, the entire copper surface showed a hilly corrosion morphology in Fig. 8(b). At this time, the Ra value of the entire Cu surface is about 70 nm. Fig. 8(a) shows the 3D morphology of the copper sample steep 0.5 M H2SO4 containing 600 mg/L psidium guajava linn leaves extract. The corrosion pits on the copper surface are obviously weakened, and the Ra value of the Cu interface is about 13 nm at this time. Therefore, it can be clearly judged that psidium guajava linn leaves extract can inhibit copper corrosion.

Fig. 10 (a) and (b) respectively show the polarization curves and the OCP diagram when the Cu electrode steep in a 0.5 M H2SO4 containing unlike concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract. As shown in Fig. 10(a), as the concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract continues to augment, the value of icorr signally shows a downward condition. This prove that psidium guajava linn leaves extract can efficaciously command the corrosion of the copper electrode after being adsorbed at the copper electrode interface. Besides, we can clearly find that the cathode branch of the polarization curves show an identical trend, which proves that the PGLLE does not change the reacting mechanism of the cathode. Fig. 8(b) is the OCP plots of copper electrodes steep at 0.5 M H2SO4 containing different concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract. After immersing for 1800 s, the open-circuit curve obviously stabilized, and at this time the entire copper interface has reached a stationary condition.

The polarization curve parameters in Table 1 are obtained using Tafel extrapolation way. The value of the icorr is calculated using formula 3 [51–54]:
(3)
η
( % )
=
i
corr , 0
-
i
corr
i
corr , 0
× 100

The icorr value of the copper electrode in the H2SO4 medium is 17.31 μA cm−2 in Table 1. The psidium guajava linn leaves extract is 50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, 200 mg/L, 400 mg/L and 600 mg/L, the corrosion current density values are 4.81μA cm−2 , 2.93 μA cm−2, 1.26μA cm−2 and 0.84μA cm−2, respectively. Among them, the largest corrosion inhibition efficiency value can reach more than 95%. Besides, the EOCP in the 0.5 M H2SO4 is − 40 mV. The maximum change in corrosion potential after adding corrosion inhibitor is less than 85 mV, so the psidium guajava linn leaves extract is a mixed-type inhibitor [55,56].

Fig. 11 (a) and (b) respectively show the Nyquist and Bode plots of copper electrodes steep in 0.5 M H2SO4 containing various concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract. As shown in Fig. 11(a), it can be found that the Nyquist diagram is composed of the capacitive loop in the high frequency region and the Warburg impedance in the low frequency district in the blank solution. The Warburg impedance in the low frequency district is caused by the diffusion of the corrosive medium at the surface of the Cu electrode into the H2SO4 solution [57]. It can be found that with the concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract aggrandize, when the concentration of PGLLE reaches 400 mg/L, it is obvious that the Warburg impedance vanish. This indicates that psidium guajava linn leaves extract forms a dense isolation film onto the copper interface, which hinders the diffusion of the corrosive particle into the bulk environment. Fig. 11 (b) is the Bode diagram of copper electrodes steep in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 containing various concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract. It can be clearly found that as the concentration of psidium guajava linn leaves extract augments, the impedance modulus graph and phase angle graph become larger and wider. This indicates that psidium guajava linn leaves extract formed a dense isolation film at the Cu surface.

In order to accurately explore the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy data, the equivalent circuit diagram (Fig. 12 ) is used to analyze the EIS results in Table 2 . The η in Table 2 is calculated using the formula (4) [17,24,58,59]:
(4)
η
( % )
=
R P
-
R
P , 0
R p
× 100

When the psidium guajava linn leaves extract reaches 600 mg/L, its η can reach 92.5%. It is also worth noting that the Cdl value and Cf value in Table 2 show a significant downward trend as the concentrations of psidium guajava linn leaves extract increases. This experimental phenomenon can be explained via formulas (5) and (6):
(5)
C
dl
=
ε 0
ε
d
S
(6)
C f
=
F 2
S
4 R T

Among them, d represents the thickness of the Cdl . Since the volume of psidium guajava linn leaves extract is significantly larger than water molecules, when PGLLE replaces the H2O molecules at the Cu surface and adsorbs on the copper surface, the d of the Cdl will be increased and the value of Cdl will decrease. In addition, S stands for the area of the Cu electrode exposed to the corrosive environment. After psidium guajava linn leaves extract is adsorbed at the Cu interface, the S value will decrease significantly, so the value of Cf will show a downward trend.

Fig. 13 (a) and (b) respectively show the polarization curve and OCP of the copper electrode in the blank solution. As the temperature increases, it is obvious that the icorr shows an augment status at Fig. 13(a), which indicates that increasing the temperatures will increases the movement frequency of the corrosion particles, which intensifies the corrosion of the copper electrode. Fig. 13(b) shows the OCP diagram of the copper electrode steep in 0.5 M H2SO4. After immersing for 1800 s, the open-circuit potential has overtly stabilized, which manifests that the surface of the copper has reached a stationary state.

Fig. 14 (a) and (b) respectively show the Nyquist diagram and Bode diagram of copper electrodes steep in 0.5 M H2SO4. In Fig. 14(a), with the test temperature augments, the capacitive loop semi-diameter at the high-frequency region is visibly reduced, which indicates that the increase in temperature accelerates the Rct speed, thereby accelerating the corrosion of the Cu. Fig. 14(b) shows the Bode plot of the copper immersed in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid. The impedance modulus plot and the phase angle plot show a downward trend with the temperature augments. This manifests that increasing the temperatures accelerates the corrosion of the Cu electrode.

Fig. 15 (a) and (b) respectively show the polarization curves and the OCP plot of a copper electrode steep in a 0.5 M H2SO4 with 600 mg/L psidium guajava linn leaves extract. As shown in Fig. 15(a), as the temperature in the solution increases, the magnitude of the icorr changes little. This shows that psidium guajava linn leaves extract can form a stabilized isolation film at the interface of the Cu electrode, thereby efficaciously control the corrosion of the Cu electrode. Fig. 15(b) shows the OCP diagram of the Cu electrode steep in H2SO4 for 1800 s. After 1800 s of soaking, the open circuit potential has obviously stabilized, which manifests that Cu electrode has reached a jar-less state.

We use Tafel extrapolation to obtain the Tafel parameters of the blank solution and containing psidium guajava linn leaves extract. These parameters are presented in Table 3 . As the temperature of the test system increases, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of psidium guajava linn leaves extract can exceed 97%. Therefore, it can be judged that psidium guajava linn leaves extract can be effectually adsorbed at the interface of the Cu electrode.

Fig. 16 (a) and (b) respectively show the Nyquist diagram and Bode diagram of copper electrodes steep in 0.5 M H2SO4 containing 600 mg/L psidium guajava linn leaves extract at diverse temperatures. As the temperature aggrandizes, it is obvious that the decreasing trend of the capacitive loop is not very obvious in Fig. 16 (a), which manifests that the isolation film formed by PGLLE on the Cu interface is relatively stable. In addition, the Bode diagram in Fig. 16(b) shows that with the temperature aggrandize, the impedance modulus diagram and phase angle diagram decline relatively small. This proves that psidium guajava linn leaves extract can still maintain high quality performance of anti-corrosion at higher temperatures.

The equivalent circuit plots (Fig. 12) is used to fit the data of EIS at different temperatures. The fitted parameter are present in Table 4 . When the temperatures are increased to 308 K and 318 K, the η of psidium guajava linn leaves extract is 94.5% and 95.9%, respectively. Therefore, this can prove that the anti-corrosion nature of psidium guajava linn leaves extract is less affected by temperature within a certain range.

In order to investigate the information of psidium guajava linn leaves extract adsorption on the copper surface. A variety of adsorption isotherms to simulate the polarization curve data. These adsorption isotherm equations including Langmuir [60–62], Temkin, Freundlich, etc., and their expressions (7)–(11) are as follows:

Langmuir:
(7)
Δ
G
ads
0

Temkin:
(8)
exp
( - 2 α θ )
= K C

El-Awady:
(9)
Δ
G
ads
0

Frumkin:
(10)
Δ
G
ads
0

Freundlich:
(11)
log θ = n log
C
inh
+ log
K
ads

The fitted data are listed in Figs. 17 and 18
. It can be clearly found that the R2 of Langmuir adsorption isotherm is observably larger than the other four adsorption models. Therefore, it can be judged that the adsorption of psidium guajava linn leaves extract on the Cu interface conforms to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model. We used formula 12 [63,64] to calculate
Δ
G
ads
0
and the Kads :
(12)

1
55.5
exp
( -

ads

RT
)

The calculated and Kads values are listed in Fig. 17. The Kads value of PGLLE adsorbed onto the interface of the Cu electrode is 45,829 L/g. The large adsorption equilibrium constant indicates that psidium guajava linn leaves extract has a large adsorption capacity at the copper interface. Besides, the value of
Δ
G
ads
0
is − 36.5 kJ/L, which manifests that the adsorption of PGLLE on the Cu surface simultaneously exists physical and chemical adsorption [65].

ce:list-item.ce:para: The infrared spectrum test shows that Psidium guajava linn leaves extract contains N, O and other heteroatoms, which has the potential of an excellent corrosion inhibitor.
Theoretical calculation results show that AGPA, DDTC, APPA, and DPCT in PGLLE can exhibit high-quality anti-corrosion nature.
The morphology test results show that Psidium guajava linn leaves extract can efficaciously control the corrosion of Cu in H2SO4 medium.

The data of electrochemical experiments show that Psidium guajava linn leaves extract is a mixed type of corrosion inhibitor, and as the temperature augment, Psidium guajava linn leaves extract can still maintain high anti-corrosion performance.
The adsorption of psidium guajava linn leaves extract at the Cu interface conforms to the Langmuir model.

ce:section.ce:para: Yundong Wu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lei Guo: Formal analysis, Visualization, Software, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Yuanbin She: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21706195, 22062022), and the Foundation of the Department of Education of Guizhou province (QJHKY2020-067, KY[2018]030).
